ISD - Change Management
definitions:
In this section an overview will be given of the used terms and definition specifically of this product. In ITIL terminology, the concept of "enabling services" is often referred to as **"supporting services"**. Supporting services are those that are necessary to deliver core services but are not directly visible to the customer. They include various IT infrastructure, applications, and processes that support the delivery of IT services. Hereby listed some of the used terms and definitions: | Terms | Definitions | | Change date field | Change Date Fields.xlsx | | Change type per impact and risk | Change type per Impact and Risk Analyses.xlsx | | RFP 0 | Predefined Change type 0.docx Including guidelines | | USD Ticket Details: definition of Statuses | USD-CMDB - USD TICKET Details |
Terms Definitions Change date field Change Date Fields.xlsx Change type per impact and risk Change type per Impact and Risk Analyses.xlsx RFP 0 Predefined Change type 0.docx Including guidelines USD Ticket Details: definition of Statuses USD-CMDB - USD TICKET Details
service_description: This document is intended for internal use by Cegeka colleagues. It is designed to help you quickly understand the enabling services within Cegeka's framework. All essential information is centralized here, with links provided for further details if required. The comprehensive setup ensures that you can start efficiently without needing to search for additional resources.
summary: SharePoint site Change & Request Management is the central hub for all your information: Change & Request Management

This includes all relevant factors that can influence the process, such as the goals, inputs, outputs, stakeholders, and constraints. Understanding the context of a process is crucial for designing, managing, and improving the process effectively. | | | A Procedure | A procedure is a set of established or prescribed methods to be followed routinely for the performance of designated operations or in designated situations. primary flow:
The change management process follows a detailed step-by-step workflow, ensuring that each change is handled systematically:
Cegeka - Change Management Process Diagram
Cegeka - Change Management Work Instructions
Visualisation of ‘to implement a change’:
implement change - eng - v2.jpg (1400×1000)
implement change - nl - v2.jpg (1400×1000)
exception – emergency change: In case of Emergency Change, only used when there is no time to adhere to the change management approval process and can’t be managed according to the normal change management process (primary flow): Cegeka - Emergency change Work instruction
specific – change models:
In some cases changes will be executed via a standardized runbook, with risk and impact that can’t be handled as a standard(ized) change. Examples include annual maintenance of firewalls or setting up a Jenkins Server for a customer environment.
The change models (‘standardized runbooks’) are listed here: Process Management - Repository - All Documents
reference to other documents:
All documents related to the change management process are located in the library: Documentation
The documents in the library are categorised as follow:
Process Classification Framework - PCF
The PCF is developed to facilitate improvement on processes and benchmarking.
All processes, activities and tasks applicable to Cegeka Infra and its description, link to process flow and documentation are searchable in the PCF of Cegeka Infra:  Process Classification Framework - Power Apps
For explanation about PCF and How to use the PCF see the presentation and recording on: Process Classification Framework - Home
cegeka multi compliance framework (mcf):
The change management process is closely linked with the Cegeka Multi Compliance Framework (MCF). The framework provides a set of controls and procedures to ensure compliance with various regulatory and operational requirements. Each change type and step in the change management process must align with the MCF to ensure that all changes are compliant and properly documented.
By adhering to the defined procedures and aligning with frameworks such as the Cegeka Multi Compliance Framework, the organization can manage changes effectively while minimizing risks and ensuring service quality.
Here you can find all controls as defined in the MCF: MultiComplianceFramework-Reports.xlsx
**For the controls of Change management: Sheet ControlView; [scope] All; Filter [process area] Request to Change.
In the following list all controls, including change management controls, where specific actions are required by the control performer for specific customers are managed, monitored and used for reporting: Controls Change management.xlsm
**Sheet Control Information. Note: not all change management controls are listed as they do not require specific actions.
Templates to be used for some of the controls:
Test plan en Test report Infrastructuur.docx
Test plan en Test report Infrastructuur_non functional.docx
Certain elements of the change management process are subject to audits to ensure compliance with the MCF. This includes the documentation of change requests, approvals, impact assessments, testing, and post-implementation reviews. The process must maintain a clear audit trail for all changes, providing evidence of compliance with the defined controls.
Emergency changes are handled through an exception procedure due to their urgent nature. These changes bypass the standard process, but certain controls must still be followed:
customer contract relationship:
The service description of a product and the Cegeka Cross Functional Description is part of the customer contract:
If a product does not utilize the Cegeka Cross Functional Description, any deviations must be clearly documented and justified.
The ownership of the service description of a product lies with the product manager, ensuring that it is updated and maintained accurately. At present, the pre-sales team manages these descriptions.
The ownership of the cross-functional description lies with service design, but a dedicated owner is required to institutionalize and standardize the process.
Any deviations prior to agreement with customer must be confirmed with the Process Office.

financial handling:
Changes handled may have different financial implications for each product and/or customer.
The decision about financial implications lies with the product manager of the product.
